g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 5 on Proximity Measures - the mathematical foundation of similarity in machine learning.
Proximity measures quantify how similar or different two data points are, forming the backbone of ML algorithms like KNN and clustering.
g.text: Proximity = how close or similar two data points are in feature space
Core concept behind classification, clustering, and recommendation systems

g.data-narration: Similarity increases when points become more alike, while dissimilarity (distance) decreases as points become closer.
Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between two points in n-dimensional space - the most commonly used metric.
g.text: - Straight-line as the crow flies distance
- Sensitive to outliers and feature scale

g.data-narration: Lets calculate Euclidean distance between points A(2,5) and B(5,9): we get 3 units difference in x and 4 in y, totaling 5.
Manhattan distance sums the absolute differences of coordinates - like navigating city blocks in a grid layout.
g.text: - Sum of absolute differences along each dimension
- More robust to outliers than Euclidean
- Used for grid-like data and categorical features

g.data-narration: For the same points A(2,5) and B(5,9), Manhattan distance equals 7 - the sum of horizontal and vertical movements.
g.text: Think: Walking 3 blocks east + 4 blocks north = 7 city blocks total

g.data-narration: Minkowski is a generalization that encompasses both Euclidean (p=2) and Manhattan (p=1) distances through parameter p.
g.text: - p - Infinity - Chebyshev Distance (max coordinate difference)

g.data-narration: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between vectors - perfect for text and high-dimensional sparse data.
g.text: - Measures angle between vectors, not magnitude
- Range: -1 to 1 (0 to 1 for non-negative data)
- Ideal for text documents, word frequencies, high-dimensional data

g.data-narration: Two documents with identical word frequency proportions (regardless of length) have cosine similarity of 1 - they are perfectly aligned.
g.text: Doc A: [2, 4, 3] words | Doc B: [4, 8, 6] words

g.data-narration: Jaccard similarity measures overlap between sets - intersection size divided by union size - perfect for binary or categorical data.
g.text: - Perfect for: market basket analysis, document similarity, binary vectors

g.data-narration: For sets A={0,1,2,5,6} and B={0,2,3,5,7,9}, the intersection is {0,2,5} (size 3) and union is {0,1,2,3,5,6,7,9} (size 8).
g.text: A Intersection B = {0, 2, 5} (size=3) | A Union B = {0,1,2,3,5,6,7,9} (size=8)

g.data-narration: Distance measures are sensitive to feature scale. A feature with larger range (like salary) dominates the calculation, causing unfair results.
Common scaling methods: Min-Max scales to 0-1, while Z-score standardizes to mean 0 and standard deviation 1.
Heres a quick reference: Euclidean for continuous data, Manhattan for robustness, Cosine for text, Jaccard for sets - choose wisely!
Remember: proximity measures define what similarity means in your model. Choose the right one based on your data type and problem context.
g.text: - Always normalize or standardize features before using distance-based methods
- Euclidean: default for continuous data | Manhattan: robust to outliers
- Cosine: orientation matters (text) | Jaccard: set overlap (binary)
- The right proximity measure can make or break your model performance!

g.data-narration: Real-world applications include KNN classification, K-Means clustering, recommender systems, and document retrieval.
g.text: - KNN and K-Means use distances | Recommender systems use Cosine | Anomaly detection uses distance-based outliers
- Image recognition (pixel vectors) | Document retrieval (TF-IDF vectors) | Market basket analysis (Jaccard)
